does: This paper introduces MAStrike, a closed-loop red-teaming framework designed to systematically identify vulnerabilities in hierarchical multi-agent systems through coordinated attacks. The core contribution is applying Shapley value analysis at the agent level to quantify each agent's marginal contribution to overall system robustness, then using these attribution scores to identify the most vulnerable agent coalitions. MAStrike generates coordinated, role-aware adversarial manipulations that exploit interactions between compromised agents, moving beyond isolated message perturbations to demonstrate how multiple agents can collude to bypass distributed safety mechanisms. The framework combines agent-level interpretability with adversarial red-teaming to systematically expose collusive attack pathways in hierarchical MAS deployed in high-stakes domains.
connects: This work builds on a convergence of research threads: multi-agent safety and robustness evaluation, which has grown as MAS see broader deployment in critical applications; Shapley value methods for interpretability and attribution in ML systems, adapting game-theoretic concepts to understand agent contributions; and red-teaming practices for identifying AI system vulnerabilities, historically focused on single-agent systems. The paper extends traditional red-teaming approaches that evaluate isolated agent behaviors or single message streams by incorporating coordination and privilege hierarchies. It responds to the observation that existing MAS safety research treats distributed defenses atomically rather than considering how role specialization creates new attack surfaces through collusion. The Shapley framework brings principled agent-level attribution to multi-agent settings, previously unexplored in this attack context.
enables: This work establishes foundations for several future directions in MAS security. It enables more sophisticated threat modeling that accounts for coordinated agent failures and privilege escalation chains in hierarchical systems, providing baselines for measuring resilience against collusive attacks. The agent-level Shapley attribution framework could extend to other MAS evaluation tasks beyond adversarial robustness, including fairness, interpretability, and bottleneck identification. Results likely motivate development of new defensive mechanisms specifically designed for hierarchical MAS that account for multi-agent coordination rather than assuming isolated failures. The closed-loop red-teaming paradigm could inspire advances in automated defense discovery and dynamic safety reconfiguration. Applications extend to real-world domains like financial trading systems and software engineering workflows where role-specialized agents operate interdependently and collusion represents a genuine threat.
